s: A labyrinth of man-made sandstone tunnels under Bristol's oldest suburb
w: Carved by hand through the soft red sandstone below Redcliffe — possibly by medieval glass-workers, possibly for wine storage, no one agrees. Pressed back into service as a public air-raid shelter in 1940. Tour guides lead you through passages that have spent longer in darkness than in use.
_facts: Series of man-made tunnels beneath the Redcliffe area of Bristol
Believed to have been excavated in medieval times, possibly by glass-workers or for wine storage
Used as a public air-raid shelter during World War II
Accessible only via guided tours from the cave entrance at Donkey Ramp

s: The 1664 pub said to have given Treasure Island and Robinson Crusoe their first breath
w: Named for the flat-bottomed Welsh trows that worked the Severn. The story goes that Defoe met the shipwrecked Selkirk here — seed of Robinson Crusoe. Stevenson supposedly modelled his Admiral Benbow on the same rooms. Bombed in 1940, still standing. Fifteen ghosts; the child on the top floor is the most heard.
_facts: Dating from 1664; Grade II* listed building on King Street
Named after Llandogo in Wales, famous for building trows (flat-bottomed cargo boats)
Said to have been where Daniel Defoe met Alexander Selkirk, inspiring Robinson Crusoe
Linked to the Admiral Benbow Inn in Robert Louis Stevenson's Treasure Island
Damaged in the Bristol Blitz during World War II but survived

s: A former snuff mill turned camera obscura with a tunnel through the cliff to a gorge cave
w: A windmill from 1766, then a snuff mill gutted by fire, rented in 1828 by artist William West for 5 shillings a year. By 1842 his camera obscura was projecting live gorge panoramas onto a bowl in a darkened room. A tunnel punched through the clifftop reaches Giant's Cave directly above the Avon. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Built as a windmill in 1766, later used to grind snuff; damaged by fire in 1777
Rented in 1828 by artist William West for 5 shillings a year; converted to an observatory by 1842
Camera obscura installed using a 5-inch convex lens to project live panoramic views
A tunnel leads from the observatory to Giant's Cave overlooking the Avon Gorge below
Grade II* listed building

s: Medieval Knifesmith Street, paved in 1669 by a vintner who named it after the Queen
w: Nothing to do with Christmas: 'knyfesmythstrete' in 1480, corrupted over time. Paved and renamed 'Queene Streete' by wine merchant Jonathan Blackwell in 1669 at his own cost; the plaque at the top records it. Royalist Colonel Lunsford was shot through the heart here in 1643 during the Storming of Bristol.
_facts: Name derives from medieval 'Knifesmith Street' (knyfesmythstrete, recorded 1480 by William Worcestre)
Paved and renamed 'Queene Streete' in 1669 by wine merchant Jonathan Blackwell at his own expense
A late-17th-century stone plaque at the top of the steps records the renaming
Royalist Colonel Henry Lunsford was shot through the heart here in 1643 during the Storming of Bristol

s: The oldest continuously working theatre in the English-speaking world
w: The Theatre Royal opened on 30 May 1766 — the oldest working theatre in Britain. The 2018 refurbishment found the original Georgian stage machinery still intact beneath the floor. Cary Grant, who grew up in Horfield, played here early on. Day-Lewis, O'Toole and Jeremy Irons all trained at the attached school.
_facts: Theatre Royal opened 30 May 1766; oldest continuously working theatre in the UK
Original Georgian stage machinery discovered during the 2018 refurbishment
Cary Grant, born in Bristol, performed here early in his career
Alumni of the Bristol Old Vic Theatre School include Daniel Day-Lewis, Peter O'Toole and Jeremy Irons

s: A sugar merchant's 1790s town house, preserved with an unsparing account of how it was paid for
w: Built c.1790 for John Pinney, who ran sugar plantations in Nevis worked by enslaved people. Kitchen, laundry, drawing room intact as in 1790. Pero Jones, Pinney's enslaved man whose name the nearby harbourside bridge now carries, worked in these rooms. The display does not look away.
_facts: Built c.1790 for John Pinney, a sugar merchant and slave-plantation owner in Nevis
Preserved as a museum furnished in late Georgian style: kitchen, dining room, study all intact
Contains a display on slavery and sugar plantations linked to Pinney's wealth
Pero Jones, Pinney's enslaved man (remembered by Pero's Bridge), worked in this house
Grade I listed; presented to the city as a museum in 1937

s: Brunel's leviathan: the first ocean-going ship to combine an iron hull with a screw propeller
w: Launched 1843; largest ship in the world until 1853; first iron steamer to cross the Atlantic, in 1845 in 14 days. Stranded in Dundrum Bay 1846, abandoned in the Falklands in 1886, and towed home to the very dry dock where she was built in 1970. Now a museum ship, afloat again in Bristol.
_facts: Designed by Isambard Kingdom Brunel; launched 19 July 1843 in Bristol
World's largest passenger ship 1845-1853; first to combine iron hull and screw propeller
First iron steamer to cross the Atlantic, completing the crossing in 1845 in 14 days
Abandoned in the Falkland Islands 1886; returned to her Bristol dry dock in 1970
Now a museum ship in the dry dock where she was built

s: A hidden funicular bored through the gorge cliff, closed since 1934, used by the BBC in the war
w: Bored through limestone at three times its estimated cost, opened in 1893 using the water-balance method: tanks filled at the top pulled the counterpart car up from Hotwells. Bankrupt 1908, closed 1934. During the Second World War the tunnel became a BBC emergency broadcast station. Occasional heritage open days.
_facts: Funded by publisher George Newnes; tunnel bored 1891-1893; propelled by water-balance, not steam
Opened 11 March 1893; carried 427,492 passengers in its first year
Went bankrupt 1908; sold to Bristol Tramways for £1,500 in 1912; last ran 1934
Tunnel requisitioned as a BBC emergency broadcast facility in World War II

s: A 105-foot sandstone tower built to mark John Cabot's 400th anniversary, still flashing Morse
w: Completed in 1898 to mark the 400th anniversary of Cabot's voyage from Bristol to what became Canada. A light at the top flashed 'Cabot Tower, Brandon Hill, Bristol' in Morse code — silenced 2001, restored 2014. Two balconied platforms, 334 feet up, command the whole city.
_facts: Built 1897-1898 to mark the 400th anniversary of John Cabot's voyage; paid for by public subscription
Designed by William Venn Gough in red sandstone; stands 105 feet (32 m) high
A Morse code light at the top was turned off in 2001 and restored in 2014
Two viewing platforms roughly 334 feet above sea level; Grade II listed

s: Bristol's oldest building, founded around 1129 by the illegitimate son of Henry I
w: Founded c.1129 by Henry I's illegitimate son Robert, Earl of Gloucester, across the Frome from the castle he was simultaneously building. The story goes every tenth Norman stone meant for the castle was diverted here. The nave survived the Dissolution because parishioners had owned it since 1374.
_facts: Founded c.1129 by Robert, Earl of Gloucester; Bristol's oldest standing building
Positioned across the River Frome from Bristol Castle, which Robert was building at the same time
Tradition says every tenth stone from Normandy for the castle was set aside for the priory
The nave survived the 1540 Dissolution due to a 1374 agreement giving it to the parishioners
Grade I listed building

s: Free live jazz and blues every night of the week on one of Bristol's oldest streets
w: Widely said to honour Duke Ellington — though the pub predates him by centuries and most likely first referred to the Duke of Cumberland. Free live music seven nights a week; an annual Jazz Festival besides. King Street outside is one of the finest surviving 17th-century streetscapes in England.
_facts: Grade II listed pub on King Street; free live jazz and blues every night
Name popularly linked to Duke Ellington though the pub predates him; likely originally the Duke of Cumberland
Hosts an annual Jazz Festival
King Street is one of the best-preserved 17th-century streetscapes in England

s: Renamed from Colston Hall in 2020, the year his statue hit the harbour
w: Built in 1867 on the site of Colston's own sugar refinery; gutted by fire in 1898 and again in 1945, the original Byzantine foyer surviving both. The Stones, The Who, Cream — all played here in the 1960s. The Colston statue hit the harbour in June 2020; the hall was renamed Bristol Beacon by September. Reopened 2023.
_facts: Concert hall opened 20 September 1867 in the Bristol Byzantine style by Foster & Wood
Site was previously Edward Colston's sugar refinery; gutted by fires in 1898 and 1945
The Rolling Stones, The Who and Cream performed here in the 1960s
Renamed from Colston Hall to Bristol Beacon in September 2020 following the toppling of the Colston statue
Reopened after a major refurbishment in November 2023

s: Bristol's oldest pub, with a front door rumoured to be wrapped in human skin
w: Dating from 1606 — the oldest surviving pub in Bristol. An 18th-century rat pit at the rear. Local legend insists the front door, under centuries of paint, is lined with human skin. No one has confirmed it; no one has quite disproved it. Grade II listed.
_facts: Dates from 1606; oldest continuously operating pub in Bristol; Grade II listed
An 18th-century rat pit operated at the rear of the building
Local legend holds the front door is wrapped in human skin under centuries of paint
Celebrated its 400-year anniversary in September 2006

s: The Victorian hydraulic works that keep Bristol's Floating Harbour from silting up
w: Jessop enclosed the tidal Avon into a Floating Harbour in 1809; silting nearly ruined it within decades. Brunel devised the underfall sluices in the 1830s to flush sediment towards the New Cut. The boatyard and pump house, rebuilt in the 1880s, still works on wooden boats today. Scheduled monument; free to walk.
_facts: The Floating Harbour was created 1804-1809 under engineer William Jessop to keep ships afloat at all states of tide
Brunel devised the underfall sluices in the 1830s to flush silt from the harbour
Yard buildings have held Grade II listed status since the 1970s; the overall site is a scheduled monument
The yard continues to maintain working boats for Bristol Harbour; free public access

s: Open since 1850, saved from demolition by a Grade II* listing, heated year-round
w: Opened in 1850, closed in 1990, earmarked for demolition until a Grade II* listing blocked it in 1998. Restored by the Bristol Glass Boat Company and reopened November 2008 with a heated outdoor pool, spa and rooftop restaurant — inside a Victorian building on a quiet Clifton terrace.
_facts: Originally opened in 1850 in Oakfield Place, Clifton
Closed in 1990; Grade II* listed in 1998, preventing demolition
Restored by the Bristol Glass Boat Company; reopened November 2008
Heated outdoor pool operates year-round alongside a spa, restaurant and roof terrace

s: Bristol's city museum in a working dockside transit shed, frank about the slave trade, free
w: Named by its old port code, M Shed occupies a 1950s transit shed on Princes Wharf with 3,000 objects on Bristol's role in the slave trade, its industry and arts. The Colston statue, pulled from its plinth and thrown in the harbour in 2020, is on display here. Transit cranes outside are Grade II listed. Free.
_facts: Located in a 1950s dockside transit shed on Princes Wharf, formerly Bristol Industrial Museum
Houses 3,000 artefacts including major displays on Bristol's role in the slave trade
The Edward Colston statue, toppled in 2020, is displayed in the museum
Transit cranes outside are Grade II listed; steam crane and harbour railway run on event days
Free admission; opened 2011

s: A Tudor lodge with an unaltered oak room — later a reformatory bankrolled by Lady Byron
w: Built 1579 as a pleasure pavilion for courtier Sir John Young; the Great Oak Room still has its Tudor carved ceiling and panelling. In 1854 Lady Byron bought it using Lord Byron's endowment and gave it to Mary Carpenter, who opened England's first girls' reformatory here. Free; easy to miss on Park Row.
_facts: Construction began 1579-1580 for Sir John Young, possibly to a design by Sebastiano Serlio
The Great Oak Room retains original Tudor carved plasterwork ceiling and wooden panelling
Bought in 1854 by Lady Byron (using Lord Byron's endowment) and given to reformer Mary Carpenter
Mary Carpenter opened the first girls' reformatory in England here in 1854
Used as a reform school until 1917; Grade I listed; free museum

s: The gateway is all that survives of the prison the 1831 Bristol rioters burned to the ground
w: Opened 1820, burned to rubble eleven years later when a crowd protesting the rejection of the Reform Bill torched it along with the Mansion House. Rebuilt but never completed; closed 1883, demolished 1898. The gateway and perimeter wall — Grade II listed — now front a housing development.
_facts: The New Gaol opened 1820, designed by Henry Hake Seward; first person hanged was 18-year-old John Horwood in 1821
Destroyed during the Bristol Riots of October 1831, when crowds burned the prison along with the Mansion House
Rebuilt but never fully completed until 1872; closed in poor condition in 1883
Demolished by the Great Western Railway in 1898; only gateway and south-east perimeter wall survive
Surviving structures are Grade II listed

s: The only grotto in Britain with both a shell room and running water, built 1737-1764
w: Hidden in the grounds of a University of Bristol hall of residence. Thomas Goldney III spent twenty-seven years building it: shells, quartz and rock crystal line the walls; inside, a Neptune statue, lion's den and fountain fed by a Newcomen engine in the gothic tower above. Occasional open days.
_facts: Built 1737-1764 by Thomas Goldney III; Grade I listed; only British grotto with both a shell room and running water
Decorated with shells, quartz and rock crystal; contains a Neptune statue, rock pool, lion's den and fountain
A gothic tower in the grounds housed what may have been the world's first Newcomen engine for non-industrial use
Goldney II provided capital for Woodes Rogers' voyage that rescued Alexander Selkirk (the real Robinson Crusoe)

s: One of the last great Gothic buildings in England, paid for entirely by Bristol's tobacco fortune
w: Commissioned 1912 by the Wills tobacco brothers as a memorial to their father, the university's first Chancellor. Oatley said the design came in a dream; halted by the First World War, opened 1925. Pevsner: 'a tour de force in Gothic Revival.' The nine-ton bell 'Great George' tolls at each Chancellor's death.
_facts: Commissioned 1912 by George and Henry Wills as memorial to their father; funded by the W. D. & H. O. Wills tobacco fortune
Designed by Sir George Oatley in Perpendicular Gothic; begun 1915, halted by WWI, opened 9 June 1925
Cost £501,566 19s 10d; 215 feet (65.5 m) tall; Grade II* listed
Nikolaus Pevsner called it 'a tour de force in Gothic Revival'
Great George bell (9.5 tons) is tolled at each Chancellor's death

s: Queen Elizabeth I called it 'the fairest, goodliest and most famous parish church in England'
w: So large that tourists routinely mistake it for Bristol Cathedral. Elizabeth I called it 'the fairest, goodliest and most famous parish church in England' in 1574. Thomas Chatterton, the teenage forger-poet who killed himself at 17, was born next door and found his medieval 'source' parchments in its muniment room.
_facts: First referenced 1158; present building dates from 1185 to 1872; Grade I listed
Queen Elizabeth I reputedly called it 'the fairest, goodliest and most famous parish church in England' in 1574
Frequently mistaken for Bristol Cathedral by tourists due to its size
Thomas Chatterton (1752-1770) was born in the schoolmaster's house next door and explored the muniment room as a child
The medieval documents Chatterton found in the muniment room inspired his forged 'Rowley' manuscripts

s: Four Elizabethan-to-Stuart bronze pillars where merchants paid on the nail — literally
w: Built 1741-43 by John Wood the Elder; the only surviving 18th-century exchange in England. Outside on Corn Street stand four bronze 'nails' — flat-topped pillars with raised rims to stop coins rolling. Oldest is Elizabethan; others dated 1594, 1625 and 1631. 'Pay on the nail' is traced here. Disputed; plausible.
_facts: Built 1741-43 by John Wood the Elder; only surviving 18th-century exchange building in England; Grade I listed
Four bronze 'nails' outside: oldest is Elizabethan; others dated 1594 (Robert Kitchin), 1625 and 1631
Merchants closed deals by paying coin on the flat-topped nails; popularly cited as origin of 'pay on the nail'
Interior plasterwork depicts Africa and America wearing tobacco-leaf headdress, symbolising slave-trade commerce
